coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials by virtue of their small size and enhanced surface area, present unique physicochemical properties that enjoy widespread applications in bioengineering, biomedicine, biotechnology, disease diagnosis, and therapy. In recent years, graphene and its derivatives have attracted a great deal of attention in various applications, including photovoltaics, electronics, energy storage, catalysis, sensing, and biotechnology owing to their exceptional structural, optical, thermal, mechanical, and electrical. Graphene is a two-dimensional sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms of atomic thickness, which are arranged in a honeycomb crystal lattice structure. Graphene derivatives are graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), which are highly oxidized and less oxidized forms of graphene, respectively. Another form of graphene is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), having a size of less than 20 nm. Contemporary graphene research focuses on using graphene nanomaterials for biomedical purposes as they have a large surface area for loading biomolecules and medicine and offer the potential for the conjugation of fluorescent dyes or quantum dots for bioimaging. The present review begins with the synthesis, purification, structure, and properties of graphene nanomaterials. Then, we focussed on the biomedical application of graphene nanomaterials with special emphasis on drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, tissue engineering, gene delivery, and chemotherapy. The implications of graphene nanomaterials on human health and the environment have also been summarized due to their exposure to their biomedical applications. This review is anticipated to offer useful existing understanding and inspire new concepts to advance secure and effective graphene nanomaterials-based biomedical devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials by virtue of their small size and enhanced surface area, present unique physicochemical properties that enjoy widespread applications in bioengineering, biomedicine, biotechnology, disease diagnosis, and therapy. In recent years, graphene and its derivatives have attracted a great deal of attention in various applications, including photovoltaics, electronics, energy storage, catalysis, sensing, and biotechnology owing to their exceptional structural, optical, thermal, mechanical, and electrical. Graphene is a two-dimensional sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms of atomic thickness, which are arranged in a honeycomb crystal lattice structure. Graphene derivatives are graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), which are highly oxidized and less oxidized forms of graphene, respectively. Another form of graphene is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), having a size of less than 20 nm. Contemporary graphene research focuses on using graphene nanomaterials for biomedical purposes as they have a large surface area for loading biomolecules and medicine and offer the potential for the conjugation of fluorescent dyes or quantum dots for bioimaging. The present review begins with the synthesis, purification, structure, and properties of graphene nanomaterials. Then, we focussed on the biomedical application of graphene nanomaterials with special emphasis on drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing, tissue engineering, gene delivery, and chemotherapy. The implications of graphene nanomaterials on human health and the environment have also been summarized due to their exposure to their biomedical applications. This review is anticipated to offer useful existing understanding and inspire new concepts to advance secure and effective graphene nanomaterials-based biomedical devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of graphene by Geim and Novoselov in 2004 [1], its demand has been steadily increasing then due to its unique properties. Graphene is a two-dimensional sheet of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms of atomic thickness arranged in a honeycomb crystal lattice structure. The single-layer graphene has a thickness of 0.335 nm [2]. Graphene sheets can be mono, double, few, or multilayer sheets [3]. One form of graphene is graphene derivatives, i.e., graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), which are highly oxidized and less oxidized forms of graphene. Another form of graphene is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), having a size of less than 20 nm. These graphene forms are considered graphene nanomaterials with unique properties owing to distinct geometry and structure [4]. They have a high surface-to-volume ratio and show various interactions with other molecules, such as π-π stacking, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions. Hence, graphene nanomaterials have been used in electrical, optical, chemical, physical, and biomedical domains [5]. Contemporary graphene nanomaterials research focuses on using them for biomedical purposes as they have a large surface area for loading biomolecules, medicine, and drugs. They also offer the potential for the conjugation of fluorescent dyes or quantum dots for bioimaging [6]. Graphene nanomaterials have excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and a maximal specific area; hence, their drug loading rate is much higher than other drug delivery systems [7,8]. Furthermore, they have outstanding thermal and electrical conductivity, optical and mechanical properties, strong fracture strength, and rapid charge carrier mobility [9,10]. Hence, they also have applications in other fields, such as nano-optoelectronics, quantum physics, catalysis, superconducting materials, bioimaging, biosensing, tissue engineering, and drug delivery [11]. These are very useful in the medical field [12] for monitoring, diagnosing, and treatment of diseases [1314] owing to their less harmful nature in mild concentrations [15], precise optoelectronic, unique physiochemical properties, and large surface area. The graphene nanomaterials can be synthesized on a large scale with low processing cost and high output, which make them easy to use in various applications [16]. Additionally, recent developments in graphene nanomaterials based biomedical equipment, including blood glucose sensors and deep brain stimulators, have made it possible to use them continuously [17,18].

After all that research, graphene nanomaterials are still a very curious and hot topic for researchers to search for more. Many biomedical applications are yet to come in the future to make human life easier and diagnosis treatment less time-consuming and more effective. The present review begins with the synthesis, purification, structure, and properties of graphene nanomaterials. Here, we have discussed the latest progress in biomedical applications of graphene nanomaterials, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensing, tissue engineering, and gene delivery. The implications of graphene nanomaterials on human health and the environment have also been summarized due to their exposure related to their biomedical applications. It is anticipated that this review will offer useful existing understanding and inspire new concepts for the advancement of secure and effective graphene nanomaterials-based biomedical devices. Additionally, many other cutting-edge materials will undoubtedly be discovered with the emergence of graphene nanomaterials, and many more futuristic technologies will become practical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and Properties of Graphene Nanomaterials

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene is a major basic unit for all graphitic forms of carbon. It has a crystalline structure and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. It is a 2-dimensional hexagonal sheet of carbon atoms of atomic thickness, arranged in the honeycomb crystal lattice structure, as shown in Fig. 1
[2]. It contains carbon atoms with a bond length of 0.142 nm. It is very flexible; hence, it can be molded into a sphere, ellipsoid, fullerene, and stacked (graphene sheets). It has a huge surface area and high thermal conductivity. It is a hydrophobic molecule because it lacks an oxygen group. Graphene shows excellent aqueous processibility, having both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties (amphiphilicity), surface-enhanced Raman scattering [19], surface functionality, and fluorescence quenching ability [20].

Graphene oxide is the only material that shows visible results in terms of large-scale production. It has a 2D crystalline structure and it is the oxidized form of graphene, which consist of various functional oxygen groups such as epoxide, carbonyl, oxygen, and phenol groups are present on the surface [21], as shown in Fig. 1 . It can be easily dissolved in water and other solvents due to the presence of the oxygen functional group. The chemical structure of GO is useful in biomedical applications owing to further chemical or biological modification.

Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [22] contains an sp2 hybridized carbon domain structure surrounded by sp3 oxygen atoms and an oxygen-containing hydrophilicity functional group, as shown in Fig. 1 . It is an intermediate between graphene and graphene oxide. It is an alternate form of GO; the only difference is that GO is more stable because intermediate can lose their electrons easily. It exhibits excellent conductivity and high stability. The presence of chemically active defective sites makes it a potential biomedical candidate.

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a size smaller than 20 nm and their structure is similar to graphene with a larger surface area, more surface functionalization, and better grafting of surface as shown in Fig. 1 . They are considered promising nanomaterials as they exhibit novel properties owing to edge effects and quantum confinement like carbon dots. They are water soluble due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxylic and hydroxyl groups at the edge, and hence, they can be modified with several inorganic, organic, and biological moieties [23,24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, Purification, and Characterization of Graphene Nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of graphene nanomaterials can be divided into top-down and bottom-up synthesis methods. Table 1 summarizes various synthesis methods for the preparation of graphene nanomaterials, viz. graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, and graphene quantum dots.

Various methods have been used for the synthesis of graphene sheets, such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD), cleavage of natural graphite [25], mechanical exfoliation, hydrogen arc discharge, plasma-enhanced CVD, microwave synthesis, and epitaxial growth of electrically insulating material, i.e., BN (boron nitride), solution-processable method [26]. 1969, J.W. et al. reported the synthesis of a single layer of graphite [27]. Following this, numerous studies have been conducted to synthesize a few layers of graphite via surface segregation of carbon at high temperatures in the presence of a metal catalyst such as Fe, Ni, Co, Pt, Pd, and Cu. Yu and his Colleagues utilized an atomically cleaned single Ni crystal surface to produce more uniform and thinner layers of graphene without grain boundaries [28]. In 2004, mechanical exfoliation of graphite was used to synthesize graphene [29]. Scotch tape has also been used to mechanically exfoliate graphite crystals [30]. This cost-effective method has been used for the explosive growth of graphene flakes, but it results in graphene flakes of a few microns in size with irregular shapes. Other approaches used to produce large-scale graphene with higher quality are the chemical exfoliation method [31], and epitaxial growth of graphene on single crystals SiC [32] wafers. To accomplish more explosive graphene growth, the CVD technique is used to synthesize graphene flakes on a large scale [33]. CVD reactions are typically catalytic reactions between hydrocarbon gases and a catalytic metal substrate. It is typically carried out in a carbon gas-containing catalytic converter. The earliest successful graphene development is achieved using the CVD technique on a Ni substrate at 1000 °C [34]. The precursor gas is degraded into atomic radicals at a particularly low temperature. This causes carbon atoms to dissolve in Ni after crystallization throughout the cooling procedure. The graphene produced by the CVD method at high temperatures exhibits negative thermal expansion when cooled down to room temperature. Hydrocarbon decomposition has also been done on the Pt and Ni surface to produce a single layer and 1–12 layers of graphene, respectively, under the ambient pressure of CVD [35,36]. Colombo and his colleagues used the CVD technique to produce large-area graphene films on Cu surface by using methane as a carbon source [37]. Similarly, bilayer graphene films have been synthesized by CVD on Cu-Ni alloy using hydrogen and methane as precursors [38]. The thickness and quality of graphene films depend on the temperature of the CVD and cooling rate. The graphene film has also been synthesized by surface aggregation of carbon atoms on Ru surface at 1400 K under an ultra-high vacuum [39]. The graphene was also prepared by low-pressure CVD on Iridium surface and Cu foil [40].

Graphene oxide is prepared either by exfoliation of graphite in solution under continuous stirring or by subjecting graphene to powerful oxidizing agents at a specific temperature, and pressure. It can be further reduced to reduced graphene oxide by chemical, thermal, photothermal, photochemical, microwave, or different microbial and bacterial methods, as shown in Fig. 2
[41] . GO is preferred in many fields due to its simpler transfer process, ease of scalability, and low cost of synthesis. There are many methods by which graphene or graphite can be converted into GO and rGO. The most common oxidation methods are the Brodie method [42], Staudenmaier method [43], Hummer’s method [44], and modified/improved Hummer’s method [45,46]. The Brodie method was the first developed method to synthesize graphene oxide by the addition of potassium chlorate (KClO3) in the mixture of nitric acid (HNO3) and graphene [42]. Nitric acid is the most commonly used oxidizing agent in a chemical reaction as it easily reacts with aromatic carbons and forms several oxides containing species such as ketones, lactones, and carboxyl. Later, a modified and improved method was introduced by Staudenmaier, which enhanced the efficiency of Brodie process by batch-feeding the KClO3 while substituting concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4) for about two third of HNO3 to enhance the acidity of reaction medium. In this method, a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 has been used as intercalant, resulting in graphene oxide formation with a high C/O ratio [43]. However, these two procedures depend on an extended oxidation process that can take up to a week. About sixty years later, an equivalent strategy was introduced as Hummer’s method [43] for synthesizing GO. In this method, the oxidation of graphite is carried by NaNO3 (sodium nitrate) and KMnO4 (potassium permanganate) with H2SO4 as intercalant. This process was completed within 2 h but with low yield, and this process also did not get much attention as it releases toxic gases (ClO2 and NOx) into the environment. Tour has modified the Hummer method by replacing NaNO3 with a 9:1 mixture of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and H2SO4. This process not only eliminate the release of toxic gases in the environment but also results in more oxidized GO with a larger sheet size as well as a regular carbon framework [45]. The final functionality in the GO depends on the type of oxidants and reaction conditions. Being hydrophilic in nature, GO can be exfoliated in various polar solvents and provide various options for industrial-scale production of chemically modified graphene. GO can also by synthesized by microwave synthesis method, which involves the reactions between graphite flakes and oxidizing agents in acidic medium under microwave irradiation [38].

Reduced graphene oxide has been produced from thermal, chemical, green, and electrochemical reduction of GO. In the thermal reduction method, the stacked structure of GO is exfoliated by heating it at a very high temperature. In this physical reduction process, CO2 gas is extruded, which builds up pressure enough to separate the stacked structures [24]. However, the electronic characteristics of GO are affected by thermal treatment due to the production of structural defects on the surface of graphene. The reduction of GO can also be carried out by the addition of reducing agents such as hydrazine, sodium borohydride, gaseous hydrogen, hydroquinone, ascorbic acid, and strong alkaline solution. However, the chemical reduction of GO is incomplete, which results in impurities in the final product [47,48]. Hence, electrochemical reduction of GO has been adopted to avoid harmful reducing agents and achieve complete reduction. This method involves deposition of GO on the surface of various substrate electrodes electrochemically. However, this method cannot scale up, and deposited rGO on the electrode surface will prevent further reduction of GO [49]. A green method has also been developed for the reduction of GO in presence of alkali solution. The green reduction of GO has been carried out by tea, amino acids, glucose, bovine serum albumin, reducing sugars, and melatonin [50-52].

A significant amount of research is being done to synthesize low-toxic GQDs using environmentally friendly alternative methods. GQDs can be synthesized by chemical ablation from graphene, which includes controlled oxidation of GO under solvothermal or hydrothermal conditions and reduction of GO by hydrazine hydrate [53-55]. They can also be synthesized by electrochemical oxidation/exfoliation of graphite in aqueous solution [56,57]. These are top-down method for synthesis of GQDs. However, they can also be synthesized by bottom-up methods such as microwave synthesis, solution processable synthesis and opening of fullerene molecule on metal surface [58-60].

Simple, quick, and efficient purification procedures are required for the purification of graphene nanomaterials for their large-scale industrial applications. However, the purification processes are expensive and time-consuming as they require repeated high-speed centrifugation, long hours of filtration, or dialysis, and also involve a large amount of water and waste generation. These standard purification methods are centrifugation, precipitation, flocculation, filtration, dialysis, and decantation [74]. Centrifugation of crude graphene nanomaterials produces their precipitates, which are dissolved in water (pH 5–7). Filtration has been carried out using polyester fiber, ceramic paper, glass wool, perlites, woven glass fiber, and celite. Flocculation and precipitation are carried by the addition of reactive solids or mineral salts. The addition of solvent combination also helps in the precipitation, filtration, and flocculation of graphene nanomaterials. In recent studies, hollow fibers have been used for diafiltration and dialysis (crossflow filtration). The pH measurements of the graphene nanomaterials dispersions are typically used to assess the efficacy of the purifying procedure. However, there is not enough research on the types of contaminants, the volume of water, and the time required for purification. Additionally, it is necessary to create new tools by which the purification process can be scaled up with high efficiency [75]. In this respect, Santos et al. developed a fluid diffusion cell to purify graphene oxide in which impurities will be diffused through the nitrocellulose membrane. The efficiency of this system has been compared with the conventional purification method. This purification method shows the best performance in removing contaminants, requires less water, and is completed in 23 h [76].

X-ray diffraction patterns determine the crystal structure of graphene nanomaterials and interlayer spacing of graphene sheets. Graphene shows three XRD peaks at 26.3° (002), 43.2° (100), and 44.6° (001), and the intensity of (002) peak determines its thickness [77]. The intense XRD peak in GO is observed around 8.4° (001) with increased interlayer spacing as compared to graphene due to intercalation of oxygen-containing functional groups between the graphene layers. The other peaks were observed at 14.9° (002), 19.9° (113), and 1.44° (102). Compared to GO, rGO shows a diffraction peak around 23.8° (002) with reduced d-spacing because of the removal of the functional groups. Hence, the structure of rGO is similar to few-layered graphite [78]. The particle size distribution and average size of graphene nanomaterials are measured by dynamic light scattering. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) is used to determine grain size and surface morphology of graphene nanomaterials [277]. The structure and morphology of the graphene nanomaterials are observed by transmission electron microscopy. Graphene oxide exhibits a wrinkled structure in contrast to the rippling structure of pristine graphene as shown in Fig. 3 because of the existence of lattice imperfections. Fourier transform-Infra red (FT-IR) spectroscopy is carried out to identify different oxygen functionalities in graphene nanomaterials [79]. The quality of graphene nanomaterials is assessed by analysing D and G bands in Raman spectroscopy. D and G bands are associated with defects and disorder in hexagonal lattice and vibration of sp2 band, respectively. The intensity ratio of these bands (D/G) provide the extent of sp2/sp3 hybridization. The Raman spectrum of graphene nanomaterials shows two distinct peaks at roughly 1340 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1, which align with the D and G bands and confirm the formation of GQDs [80,81,277].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility and biocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials
Cytocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanomaterials have drawn a lot of attention in all areas of science and technology. Various graphene nanomaterials have been synthesized in terms of number of layers, size of graphene sheets, and surface functionalization, which impart different chemical, physical and biological properties. Hence, it is essential to understand in vitro cytocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials to further investigate their in vivo biocompatibility and develop biomedical devices based on them, which has been schematically represented in Fig. 4 . The physicochemical features of graphene nanomaterials, such as size, number of sheets, surface functionalization, and hydrophobicity, along with their concentration, influence their cytocompatibility and biocompatibility [82].

GO, rGO and GQDs were found to be less toxic than graphene because of hydrophilic nature of GO and rGO and small size of GQDs. It has been observed that low concentration of GO (less than 10 µg/ml) did not show significant reduction in the viability of human fibroblast cells even after exposure for 4 days. However, the higher concentration of GO (more than 50 µg/ml) exhibited cytotoxic effect after exposure for 1 day owing to agglomeration of GO layers in the physiological medium via π-π stacking [83]. The aggregated GO cannot enter the cell and hence, is trapped on the cell membrane, which results in distortion of the cell membrane, disruption of the cell and enhancement of intracellular stress, all of which lead to cell death. This situation is also applicable to other graphene nanomaterials. Hence, graphene nanomaterials should be stable in physiological media of body without aggregation for biomedical applications. The long-term stability and enhanced cytocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials can be achieved by proper functionalization of their surface. In this respect, cytotoxicity of pristine graphene has been reduced by carboxyl functionalization and it has been observed that 300 µg/ml concentration of functionalized graphene did not show significant reduction in the viability of vero cell after exposure of 1 day [84]. The hydrophilic functionalization of graphene nanomaterials can enhance the cytocompatibility but production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in mammalian cell cannot be avoided, which is their major drawback in biomedical application [85]. The hydrophilicity can also be achieved during synthesis of graphene nanomaterials. The hydrophilic graphene nanomaterials also promote cell adhesion and hence, they have been used as substrate for various cells [86].

The size and number of graphene layers in graphene nanomaterials also affect their cytocompatibility. The smaller size graphene nanomaterials are more cyto-compatible as compared to larger sized graphene nanomaterials. Haynes and co-workers have studied the hemolytic activity of human erythrocyte cells by different sized graphene and GO [87]. They have observed that smaller size graphene nanomaterials exhibited better hemolytic activity than larger-sized graphene nanomaterials. Graphene showed less hemolytic activity as compared to GO owing to their tendency to aggregate more in the physiological medium. The cell capture and adhesion properties of graphene nanomaterials can also be enhanced by oxygen clustering. Bardhan et al. enhanced the cell capture ability of functionalized GO via oxygen clustering [88].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: It is essential to investigate in vivo biocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials before utilizing them in biomedical applications. There are currently very few reports on the in vivo biocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials. Their biocompatibility also depends upon their physiochemical properties along with the concentration.

For the first time, Koyakutty and co-workers studied the biodegradability of pristine graphene with the help of confocal Raman imaging [89]. The aggregates of pristine graphene have been identified in mice's liver, kidney, spleen, and lungs owing to the development of structural disorder in macrophage-phagocytized graphene. Hence, it has been suggested that biodegradability of graphene has a strong influence on their biological and medical applications. Size and dose-dependent in vivo toxicity of GO in mice concluded that GO accumulated in lungs and lived for longer time compared to other body organs due to nonspecific binding of GO towards different proteins [90]. The effect of GO and rGO has been studied on blood platelets, and it has been observed that GO aggregated more in platelets as compared to rGO. This is related to the charge distribution of GO on the surface because aggregation tendency of GO reduced after converting into rGO. Furthermore, they discovered that giving GO to mice intravenously produces pulmonary thromboembolism. [89] Langer and his colleagues observed that GO is moderately biocompatible in both subcutaneous and intraperitoneal tissue site after testing with two different oxidation states [91]. Mutlu and co-workers showed that the covalent oxidation state of graphene decides its pulmonary toxicity [92]. This pulmonary toxicity of graphene and GO can be reduced by liquid phase exfoliation, which can be further reduced by dispersing them block copolymer Pluronic F 108NF. Hence, pluronic offers a method for the secure handling of graphene nanoparticles and their prospective use in biomedical applications. GO also exhibited good in vivo biocompatibility with red blood cells. Additionally, biocompatibility of graphene and GO can be enhanced by altering their size and functionalizing them with non-toxic biomolecules during their synthesis. The degree of oxidation in GO is another factor that is crucial in determining their biocompatibility.

Graphene nanomaterials show great response in the interactions with DNA and RNA due to rapid response in detecting, sensing, and delivery of DNA and RNA. As DNA is double-stranded and hence, it is difficult to adsorb double-stranded DNA. For single-stranded DNA, GO is a good adsorbent. During the adsorption process, tissues may wear and tear, so GO provides a vast opportunity to protect the nuclease enzyme by adsorbing nucleotides [93]-[94]. DNA carries a negative charge, which helps to increase the ionic strength through adsorption process and decreases its pH [95]. Titov et al. [96] show that graphene is stable and helps in making stable hybrids of functionalized proteins and lipids. They also oppose the interaction of DNA and RNA, but no specific mechanism or study has been found yet that they oppose it. It confirmed that they oppose the interactions of graphene with proteins and lipids. For this confirmation, experimental data is needed which can be proved. It is important to understand the interaction of graphene in the cell membrane with the lipid bilayers.

Graphene nanomaterials are non-biodegradable, which causes hazards in the environment and side effects in humans, such as lung toxicity. Research has been conducted by Kotchey et al. [97] in which they confirmed that GO shows biodegradation by the oxidative attack of hydrogen peroxide. At the same time, reduced graphene oxide contradicts this biodegradability effect. It has been observed that graphene and rGO also show anti-bacterial activities. They are used for the prevention of growth of Escherichia coli and staphylococcus aureus bacteria. However, rGO has great effectiveness in anti-bacterial activities and shows no inhibition of bacterial growth [98]. More exploration is needed on the rGO effect on the surface of oxygen in cellular interaction. Qu et al. [99] proved that macrophages undergo necrotic cell death by GO. Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) signalling activation and subsequent autocrine TNF production are both involved in this toxicity. Similarly, size-dependent absorption methods of protein-coated graphene oxide nanosheets, subcellular locations, and cell surface adherence have been studied to facilitate the understanding of interaction of biological system with two-dimensional nanosheets [100]. Small nanosheets mostly enter cells by clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and as graphene size increases, the nanosheets are more readily taken up by phagocytes. Wu and co-workers highlighted the crucial role of surface physicochemical properties in the interaction of graphene and nanomaterials with biocomponents. They demonstrated that moderately reduced few-layer graphene oxide shows best performance for cell adhesion, growth, and phenotype [101]. For metal reduction, rGO has been used with the bacteria family named Shewanella [102] . Thus, graphene nanomaterials can be used in various external injuries or wound healing properties to prevent infection.

Now, it can be concluded that the shape, size, number of graphene layer, and hydrophobicity of graphene nanomaterials play an important role in determining their cell interactions, intercellular uptakes, and cell penetration.

Graphene nanomaterials are used in many biomedical applications, which deal with drug delivery [103], gene delivery [104], biosensing [105], tissue engineering, and bioimaging. Graphene is supposed to be a “wonder” material for the therapeutic field. Its derivatives are also used in therapeutics, treatments, and diagnosis, such as GO and rGO, which built more interest in this field. These are also helpful in the regeneration of tissue engineering [106], and reconstruction of the bone defect [107,108]. However, they cause high-cost grafting, lack of viable cells, immunogenicity risk, infection transmission, and synthetic bone based on ceramics, which help in enhancement of their properties. Graphene nanomaterials are also used in mechanical and osteogenic properties in the dental field. Various biomedical application of graphene nanomaterials, as shown in Fig. 5 , has been discussed in this review paper.

Chemotherapy is the most used approach for treating cancer. It is the most adaptable process worldwide in clinical applications. It is the most preferred due to its broad spectrum and great efficiency. The most common anti-cancer drugs are DOX [109] and CPT [110], paclitaxel [111], SAN, resveratrol and vincristine, and methotrexate[112]. These are loaded with carrier in drug delivery by pi-pi stacking containing amide bonds, which are insoluble in water and have poor biocompatibility in biomedical applications. Anti-cancer drugs also suffer from unclear tumor targeting, rapid elimination through metabolism, and short blood circulation time. They an amazing effect on breast cancer; lung cancer and pancreatic cell as it inhibits tumor growth by approximately 60–90% [113]. The drug delivery to specific cells or tissue is very important for curing diseases. In recent years, smart and biocompatible theragnostic system has been created to deliver drugs at specific sites, protect them from biodegradation, and solubilize these drugs efficiently. In this respect, graphene nanomaterials-based system has been developed for effective and targeted delivery of drugs. Functionalized GO improves the physiochemical properties and enhances the effect of these drugs on cancer cells [114]. Hence, researchers are working on modification of graphene nanomaterials to increase their effectiveness for in vivo chemotherapeutic applications and in vitro cancer cell testing. The application of graphene nanomaterials in various drug delivery has been summarized in Table 2 .

Zhang et al. developed folic acid functionalized GO (FA-GO) for controlled loading and targeting delivery of mixed anticancer drug. The folic acid stabilized the graphene oxide in the physiological medium. FA-GO was loaded with two anti-cancer drugs, doxorubicin (DOX) and camptothecin (CPT), via hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking [120]. Chen and co-workers reported the delivery of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DXR) with high loading capacity of 1.08 mgmg−1 by superparamagnetic GO-Fe3O4 nanoparticles hybrid [132]. Leu et al. synthesized biocompatible polyethylene glycol functionalized GO (PEG-GO), which is stable in various solutions. These were noncovalently loaded with SN38 to kill colon cancer cells [132]. PEG-GO has also been used for intracellular DXR delivery [133]. Misra and co-workers developed a drug delivery system with high drug loading capacity for controlled drug release, which has strong pH dependence and hydrogen bonding interaction between DOX and GO. In this system, GO is loaded noncovalently with DOX, following the encapsulation of GO by folic acid conjugated chitosan [134]. Pan et al. synthesized water-soluble and biocompatible poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) - graphene sheets for loading water-insoluble CPT drug with a loading capacity of 15.6 wt%. These sheets had high potency of killing in vitro cancer cells [135]. Graphene nanosheet–carbon nanotube–iron oxide nanoparticle hybrid were loaded with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) anticancer drug with loading capacity of 1.08 mgmg−1 and delivered this drug into liver cancer cells [133]. For more precise target delivery of anticancer drugs, Chen et al. synthesized SiO2 quantum dots conjugated graphene hybrid nanocomposites, which were functionalized with transferrin (Trf) ligands. Trf ligands are iron-binding proteins that have a greater affinity to overexpressed Trf receptors on the surface of cancer cells [136]. In addition to targeted drug delivery, triggered drug delivery has recently gained a lot of attention as these systems allow for precise drug dosage control and instantaneous adjustments to dosing schedules based on the physiological reaction of the user. In this respect, remote controlled drug delivery system has been designed by embedding photoacid generator (PAG) into GO capped mesoporous silica nanocomposite [137] as shown in Fig. 6 . These DOX loaded nanocomposite showed 80 % selectivity towards HeLa cells. In particular, PAG molecules were embedded on the mesoporous silica nanoparticles, that are conjugated with boronic acid (BA) via strong physical adsorption. Then, these nanoparticles were capped by an acid labile GO sheets to create a nanogate type assembly. Kim et al. functionalized rGO with PEG and branched polyethylene amine (BPEI) polymer and reported the photothermal triggered DOX delivery via endosomal disruption. PEG-BPEI-rGO showed greater cancer cell death efficiency in presence of NIR radiation as compared to without irradiation [138].

Wang et al. reported the synthesis of FA-capped GQDs for delivery of anticancer drug, i.e., DOX. FA-capped GQDs were multifunctional as these can also be utilized for cell imaging and real time monitoring of cellular uptake along with drug delivery [139]. Iannazzo et al. synthesized GQDs from oxidation and exfoliation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and then used them to deliver DXN by conjugating with cancer targeting module biotin [140]. Similarly, a graphene quantum dot-based FRET system and aptamer/GQDS nanocomposite-capped silica nanoparticles have been utilized for real-time monitoring of delivery of drug [141,142]. In summary, the development of graphene nanomaterial has improved the loading efficiency of drug delivery along with their real-time monitoring.

Although the use of graphene nanomaterials for the administration of drugs has been extensively researched, very few studies concentrate on utilizing graphene nanomaterials for gene delivery. Gene delivery is similar to drug delivery (delivery of small molecule). Still, it is specifically used to deliver foreign DNA into the cell and is a promising method for treating genetic disorders. Finding effective and secure gene vectors that shield DNA from nuclease degradation and promote its absorption with high transfection efficiency is essential for gene therapy. Gene delivery is carried out by either a viral vector or non-viral vector. Viral vector imposes many challenges for their clinic use, and non-viral vectors are safer and more adaptable [143]. Hence, the most admirable gene therapy that is treated well for genetic disorders is non-viral gene therapy [144]. In this therapy, surface of the deposited solid phase or bulky liquid phase is used to transfer cells with new genes. Initially, gene vehicles or gene vectors must be loaded with DNA and then, verified before transfection with genes. However, gene expression and transfection efficiencies are low for non-viral vectors. In this respect, graphene nanomaterials are perfect non-viral vector for gene delivery owing to their unique physiochemical properties as shown in Fig. 7 . Graphene nanosheets have low cytotoxicity and a higher stability vector, which helps in easy uptake of cells and [145]. They exhibit improved penetration of siRNA (Small interfering RNA) or pDNA (plasmid DNA) into cells [115]. Graphene nanomaterials show covalent and non-covalent interactions with nucleic acids, DNA, and RNA [146]. They exhibit high loading of genes and increase the gene transfection efficiency by incorporating cationic polymer, i.e., PEI (polyethyleneimine) [147]. The cationic polymer enhances the interaction of graphene nanomaterials with negatively charged DNA and cell membrane via electrostatic attraction [148]. Additionally, the “proton sponge” effect caused by the positive charges of PEI makes it easier for the cargo to exit the endosome [149]. Similarly, polymers such as chitosan, PAMAM (polyamidoamide), and PEG have also been used to modify graphene nanomaterials. These polymers aid in the modification of the surface of the graphene nanomaterials, hence making them suitable for use in gene delivery via covalent conjugation and electrostatic interaction for gene delivery.

Chen et al. and Feng et al. employed PEI functionalized-GO for gene therapy as a non-viral gene delivery vector with higher transfection efficiency. They have demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of the PEI-GO complex [150,151]. Sequential delivery of Bcl-2 targeted siRNA and DOX into Hela cells was carried out by PEI-GO complex with lower cytotoxicity and higher transfection/anti-cancer efficiency [152]. Plasmid DNA and PEG-BPEI-rGO were complexed for photothermally regulated gene delivery. PEG-BPEI-rGO nanocomposites did not exhibit cytotoxicity towards PC3 and NIH/3T3 cells. rGO accelerated endosomal membrane breakdown under NIR radiation, which led to higher transfection efficiency [153]. Chitosan functionalized GO has been specifically designed for anti-cancer medication and delivery of loaded pDNA delivery via π-π and electrostatic interaction [154]. Chitosan provides biocompatibility and aqueous solubility to the chitosan-GO composite. Hela cells exfoliate more than A549 cells, i.e., adenocarcinomas human alveolar basal epithelial cells. Its efficiency in exhibiting cells is higher when accumulated with FGNs (functional graphene nanomaterials). Hence, FGNs become a promising compound for gene delivery [155]. Similarly, PEG-GO and PEI-GO have been used to deliver EGFP-coding pDNA and plasmid-based stat3 siRNA [156,157]. PAMAM dendron conjugated GO has been synthesized for enhanced transfection efficiency and improved DNA complexation ability [158]. For simultaneous intracellular microRNA (miRNA) imaging and gene therapy, a poly(L-lactide) (PLA) and PEG grafted GQDs-based multifunctional nanocomposite has been developed [159]. Single-stranded ribonucleic acid (ssRNA) has been successfully loaded and delivered by PEG-rGO [156]. Xu et al. encapsulated Au nanoparticles and nanorods in GO via electrostatic interaction. Cytocompatibility of this system has been enhanced by surface functionalization with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide and PEI. The functionalized composite demonstrated 65 % transfection efficiency and 90 % viability of Hela cells [160]. Table 3 represents the summary of gene delivery application of graphene nanomaterials.

In conclusion, despite the development of gene delivery systems based on graphene nanomaterials that have improved gene loading efficiency and in vitro intercellular delivery, a lot more in vivo research needs to be done to validate the in vitro research before moving forward with clinical applications in the future.

One of the most important biological applications of graphene nanomaterials is biosensing. Biosensors evaluate the spectrochemical, electrochemical, or magnetochemical behaviour of the systems in order to qualitatively or quantitatively identify the particular type of analytes/biomolecules. The detection of biomolecule assists in the diagnosis and therapy, which can be achieved by graphene nanomaterials owing to their superior electrochemical and optical characteristics. Graphene nanomaterials can interact with adsorbed analyst via electrostatic or π-π interaction. Hence, graphene nanomaterials-based biosensors have been used to detect small chemical molecules like glucose and hydrogen peroxide as well as biomolecules such as thrombin, adenine triphosphate (ATP), oligonucleotides, amino corrosives, nicotinamide, and dopamine. Biosensors contain transducers (electronic components) and receptor, which is used for the determination of biological responses. These biological responses will then transform into an electrical signal with the assistance of a transducer. Here, we are going to discuss electrochemical, fluorescence, and field effect transistors-based biosensors of graphene nanomaterials, which are summarized in Table 4
.

Electrochemical biosensors recognize various biomolecules such as antibodies, enzymes, peptides, and oligonucleotides by transforming the chemical signal into the electrochemical signal by particular recognition. The electrochemical signal is proportional to the analyte concentration. Electrochemical biosensors have high specificity, depending on the type of utilized materials. Electrochemical biosensors are also robust, easy to miniaturize and offer broad detection limits even when only small volumes of analyte are present. Furthermore, electrochemical biosensors can be used to analyze biofluids with high turbidity and optically absorbing and fluorescing compounds [189]. A schematic diagram of working electrode of electrochemical biosensors has been shown in Fig. 8 . A typical electrochemical sensor consists of a sensing/working electrode and a reference electrode, which are separated by an electrolyte. For most of the applications, a three-electrode system is used with the reference electrode, which is connected to a high-input-impedance potentiostat and a counter electrode is used to complete the circuit for current. The detection methods for electrochemical transducers include amperometry, which is based on measuring the current of electroactive species. The current is directly proportional to the concentration of the electroactive species. These sensors are based on the reduction or oxidation of biomolecules, which takes place at the edges or corners of graphene nanomaterials [190]. The enzymes are immobilized on the surface of graphene nanomaterials for a heterogeneous electron transfer process and produce reduced b-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) or oxidizable hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [191]. The large surface area of graphene nanomaterials makes them conductive, which guarantees a highly sensitive transfer of electrons. Glucose, cholesterol, uric acid (UA), NADH, H2O2, ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), and metal ions have been detected electrochemically by graphene nanomaterials based biosensors [180,184,192-194].

The performance of graphene nanomaterials-based biosensors can be enhanced by modification of their hydrophilic surface by semiconductor nanomaterials, metal nanoparticles, quantum dots, and metal oxide nanoparticles. In this respect, a glassy carbon electrode modified by PVP-graphene has been developed for the simultaneous detection of AA, UA, and DA. The detection limits for AA, UA, and DA were decreased to 0.8 M, 0.02 M, and 0.002 M, respectively [195]. The sensor has been used to successfully assess real samples owing to its exceptional sensitivity, adjustable reproducibility, great selectivity, and long-range stability.

The field effect transistor (FET) biosensors are based on a change in conductivity and charge density, which follows the binding of the sensor with the analyte. It results in signal transduction at the end. Graphene nanomaterials are the best choice for biosensors owing to their zero-band gap and charged backbone [196]. Hence, graphene nanomaterials-based FET biosensors detect various biomolecular analytes such as glucose, protein, and DNA with high precision, efficiency, specificity, label-free and real-time detection. rGO is the most preferred FET biosensor material among various graphene nanomaterials because of their higher conductivity and superior surface functional characteristics. The sensitivity of these biosensors can be enhanced by modification of the surface of graphene nanomaterials by metal nanoparticles, which lead to the detection of very small concentration i.e., 10 nM of single-stranded DNA [187].

Optical biosensors based on graphene nanomaterials are also known as fluorescence biosensors. The fundamental components of optical biosensors are complementary sensors with respect to analyte, fluorophore, and graphene nanomaterials as fluorophore carriers. The mechanism of the optical biosensor [197] has been represented schematically in Fig. 9 . It involves the fluorescence quenching owing to the non-covalent interaction of fluorophore-labeled probe with graphene nanomaterials. Then, the analyte is bound to the ligand, which results in altered conformation of the probe; hence, probes are separated from graphene nanomaterials. The fluorophore's absence of the quenching carrier restores fluorescence intensity and amplifies the signal from the detecting process. The fluorescence intensity is directly proportional to the concentration of the analyte. The detection sensitivity depends on the distance between the fluorophore and the quenching carrier. Optical biosensors based on graphene nanomaterials have been used to detect medicinal substances, cytokines, proteins, antigens, biomolecules, and antibodies [27,178,198].

Bioimaging is taking a huge place in biomedical applications for a better understanding of simple as well as complex biological processes in the cells, tissues, and whole organisms of the body [199]. Additionally, it will also help in the identification of abnormal processes that lead to cancer and other diseases. It is used to monitor problems for both in vitro and in vivo environments, which helps in the development of new techniques and tools [200]. However, conventional bioimaging application suffers from poor specificity, sensitivity, and targeting. Hence, it is essential to develop more efficient bioimaging techniques for various applications. Graphene nanomaterials especially GQDs are used for bioimaging purposes owing to their unique physiochemical properties. In general, iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) mixed with graphene oxide are utilized for bioimaging and loaded with anti-cancer drugs i.e., DOX and PEG [201]. However, many studies have proved that graphene can cause toxicity, so utilization of graphene, GO and rGO in bioimaging remains difficult. On the other hand, GQDs are the most reliable graphene derivative for bioimaging applications [202]. In 2000, GQDs have been prepared by cutting graphene sheets hydrothermally, which show notable physical properties. There is a continuous need for improvisation for bioimaging agents and develop specialized bioimaging probes. Photoacoustic imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, optical (fluorescence) imaging, and Raman spectroscopy are commonly used imaging techniques. The bioimaging applications of graphene nanomaterials have been summarized in Table 5
.

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is a noninvasive imaging technique that absorbs electromagnetic waves via contrast agent under low-energy non-ionizing laser pulse illumination. Then, it converts light waves into sound waves, excites broadband sound waves through local heating, and eventually collects sound waves for visual processing. Because of its great spatial resolution and deep organ/tissue imaging, PAI is considered one of the most promising imaging approaches in the biomedical industry. Additionally, melanomas and blood vessels are visible with excellent spatial resolution. However, endogenous contrast substances like melanin and haemoglobin can easily affect imaging outcomes. To attain excellent imaging performance via PAI, good contrast agents are crucial.

Graphene nanomaterials act as contrast agents in PAI with larger sp2 domains as they absorb near infra-red (NIR) efficiently. Because of their strong ability to show NIR optical absorbance, they allow for higher spatial resolution in deep tissues and organs than optical imaging. Among graphene nanomaterials, rGO act as prominent PAI contrast agent because of effective NIR absorbance larger sp2 domains. However, rGO has low biocompatibility owing to less hydrophilicity. As PAI is helpful in the scanning of tumors from deep tissues or organs which are administrated with rGO. After administration of rGO, its PA signals automatically increase in tumors, which makes them easier to penetrate deeper. PA signals are generated by thermal expansion due to the low scattering of light. Piras et al. reported the PAI of the breast by using the Twente Photoacoustic Mammoscope. PAI absorbs electromagnetic energy and converts it into heat, which results in thermal expansion [214]. Due to thermal expansion, it can easily absorb acoustic waves and light waves and carry out interconversion between them. The use of rGO as contrast agent in in vivo PAI has been find out to be very beneficial as it provides PA images with accurate spatial resolution and low background noises. Liu and his co-workers concluded that rGO-Au nanoribbons show great response in clinical applications for PA images of tumor therapy [125]. The probes based on rGO and IONP along with PEG results in better photoacoustic tomography. Chen and his group studied that the use of GO/Au nanocomposite as contrast agent provides sensitive PA images when loaded with anti-cancer drugs via pi-pi stacking [215]. GO/Au nanocomposites helped in determining the precise location, size, sharpness, and morphology of the tumor. Microwave-enabled low oxygen graphene (ME-LOGr) has been developed by Patel et al. [216], which show strong and wavelength independent visible as well as NIR absorption as shown in Fig. 10
. The absorption co-effecient of ME-LOGr was greater than indocyane green, which is best NIR fluorophore dye and hence, provides clear PA signal. The role of graphene nano-ribbons as photo-acoustic agents in PAI has been shown by Lalwani et al. [210]. Graphene nano-ribbons can either be a single layer or multilayers, which were used in the PAI for enhanced results. Au NRs have lesser amplitudes than hybrid systems, but reduced GO-AuNRs or silica-coated Au NRs can be used in both in vivo and in vitro systems. Sheng et al. demonstrated that nano-sized rGO combined with BSA can be used as a PA agent in both in vivo and in vitro PAI [217]. For further alteration, Paradossi and his group show the use of pristine graphene as a PA agent for in vivo experiments [218]. They use it with PVA-shelled micro-bubbles with acoustic contrast. They also found that graphene can be used for deep tissue imaging in tumor or cancer cases. Patel et al. obtained the less oxygenated nanosheets by the reduction method. These sheets have been used after stabilizing them by BSA via a one-step reduction method, which resulted in higher stability and low toxicity [216]. The BSA functionalized GO with a lateral size of ∼ 70 nm show poor monodispersity as compared to 10 nm lateral size. In conclusion, graphene nanomaterials have a lot of potential for PAI applications.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is highly sensitive and non-invasive technique that offers high spatial resolution and deep tissue penetration. It is similar to PAI, the only difference is that it is a non-radiation technique whereas PAI is a completely radiation-dependent method. According to Reviazhang et al., radiation may cause some toxic effects in the body after some time. MRI is nontoxic technique but time-consuming [219], long operational as well as have low sensitivity for about ∼ 10-3 – 10-5 mol/L. In MRI, T1 and T2 represent longitudinal and transverse relaxation time, respectively. This technique needs time and many attempts to improve the relaxation time of T1 and T2 to observe the better contrast. Gd3+ (gadolinium) and Mn (manganese) are used as T1 contrast agents and iron oxide as T2 contrast agents. T1 is basis for getting brighter images and T2 is based on darker images. These contrast agents may have more or less toxic effects in non-selective coordination. Hence, graphene nanomaterials are used as contrast agents because of their ability to chelate with harmful paramagnetic ions, which reduces the toxicity.

Among the graphene nanomaterials, GQDs show excellent performance in imaging due to their good solubility, less toxicity, easy functionalization, intrinsic fluorescence, and biocompatibility. Lee et al. developed novel Mn-N and Gd-N co-doped GQDs as dual imaging agents for MRI and fluorescence imaging [220]. Additionally, Li et al. controlled the local superacid microenvironment of PEGlated-GQDs (PGQDs) loaded with Gd3+ to obtain low in vitro cytotoxicity in vivo biocompatibility. The folic acid-modified PGQDs have been shown to be effective for MRI as well fluorescence imaging in mice with a specific cellular uptake rate of greater than 98.3 % [221]. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) functionalized with Gd-1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetra acetic acid complex can be used to make paramagnetic GQDs [222]. The highest longitudinal relativity chain has been obtained by GQD-PEG12-Gd, which is 16 folds greater than a commercial MRI agent. Other than paramagnetic complexes, non-magnetic MRI contrast agents have also been developed. IONPs and fluorinated GO have been used as MRI contrast agents by creating a C-F dipolar bond as paramagnetic centres. Another agent that shows great results is B-GQD [223]. It is time-dependent study and enhances the performance in in vivo system. These are safer as compared to others and standalone with other graphene-based contrast agents, which holds great potential in the clinical application of MRI. Dual-mode imaging yields more trustworthy data and boosts the capacity to visualize images than single-mode imaging, which enhances the accuracy and effectiveness of MRI. Additionally, it broadens the scope of applications for graphene nanomaterials in bioimaging.

Tissue engineering is a new emerging biomedical field, which combines the expertise of engineering, electrochemistry, and medicine [103]. The focus of tissue engineering is to develop biodegradable substitute for repairing and maintaining the properties of tissues/organs or modification of their functions. The ability of traditional transplantation to treat tissue damage brought on by trauma, infection, tumours, and deformity is limited. Materials like hydrogels lack the necessary mechanical strength for cells attachment and propagation [224]. Additionally, the mechanical, electrical, or physical properties of various tissues vary with each other. Single materials cannot perform all functions and hence, hybrid bioactive materials with a variety of components are required for meeting different criteria, which are frequently utilised to fabricate artificial tissues. This technique is useful in various biomedical applications such as quick wound healing, regeneration medicine, and stem cell engineering [225]. For mimicking the biological environment, several biomaterials have been developed for maintaining the parameters of tissue engineering. For differentiation of cells, it provides a large surface area to cells interface, enabling cell attachments, and proliferation. It consists of compound-name hydrogels, which are viscous elastic and result in good imitate property of transport of cell in natural times. This property prevents the advance use of tissue engineering applications. As a result of less use, mechanical qualities like strength, shape, and size may be compromised [226]. Hence, graphene nanomaterials can be used for tissue engineering application as they have good mechanical qualities, improve the products mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, and stiffness. These properties make the compound more accurate to give good results. Graphene nanomaterials show remarkable interest in the field of tissue engineering applications [227]. Table 6 summarizes the tissue engineering application of graphene nanomaterials.

One of the applications in which graphene can be taken legal action as a reinforcement material in hydrogels, electro spunfibers, biodegradable films, and many other tissue engineering scaffolds. For more enhancement of tissue engineering, it gets combined with graphene derivative that is graphene oxide. The PVA in graphene oxide [228], helps to maintain its stability and enhance its tensile strength and compressive strength [229]. Its cytocompatibility will remain the same while it differs in the strength range. Its tensile strength will vary by 132 % and compressive strength will by 36 %. For the construction of bone tissue, Bai, R. G., and his group prepared 3D GO/HA hydrogel as bone tissue engineering [230]. Because it is highly porous, it is mechanically very strong, has a high electrical conductivity property, and is cell compatible with MSCs (mesenchymal stem cells). It results out to be a remarkable product for the bone tissue engineering application. For more improvement of mechanical properties, graphene nanosheets are taken with gelatine hydrogel scaffolds/ PAA (poly acyclic acid) [231]. The Chitosan amino acid group and the carboxylate group of graphene show a covalent bond with Chitosan-GO scaffolds which are prepared by covalent linkage [232].

Saburiet et al. have used electrospinning methods for showing the presence of graphene in chitosan poly (vinyl alcohol) nano-fiber [248]. They found that rapid healing of wounds in mice and rabbit-reinforced fibers shows a great result. It is because the free electrons are present in graphene, which slows down the multiplication of prokaryotic cells [249] without making any stumbling block in eukaryotic cells [250]. Other than wound healing thing, many studies have been done on musculoskeletal tissue engineering [251] and stem cell engineering[252].Shang and his co-workers studied graphene and graphene oxide platforms [253]. The development of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC) will be aided by them. For the grant of spontaneous differentiation Hossein Zadeh and his co-workers showed that graphene supports iPSC and for proliferation and more differentiation graphene oxide shows belief in iPSC [248]. Thus, Graphene coated scaffolds show varying stiffness and roughness which is useful in the evaluation differentiation of human stem cells. For direct iPSC differentiation graphene oxide-coated scaffolds are used [254]. Purohit and co-workers used gelatines to functionalize graphene oxide (GO-Gel) for the biometric mineralization of HA [255]. Osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E cells [256], proliferation [257], cell adhesion [258], and promotion in all these can be done with the use of GO-Gel. Bone tissue engineering scaffolds can also be improved by using GO-Gel to promote osteogenesis. Kuznetsova and his co-workers give the same results but use different materials, they use graphene oxide with polysaccharide carrageenan [259]. Lin and his team show the bone marrow culture, and how they derived MSCs (mesenchymal stem cells) on graphene and graphene oxide substrate [260]. For the more growth of MSCs, a pro-concentration platform for osteogenic inducers is the bonding between electrostatic and hydrogen bonding. For non-covalent binding abilities of graphene from pi-pi stacking allows it to act as a pro-concentrated platform. This will help to increase their growth and stability. The adhesion and differentiation of human neural stem cells (hNSCs) have been enhanced by coating laminin on graphene sheets as shown in Fig. 11
[261].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human health risks and environmental effects of graphene nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanomaterials are used in various biomedical fields such as drug delivery, gene delivery, tissue engineering, bioimaging and biosensing. However, it is very important to understand the considerable variation in graphene nanomaterials before implementing their use in human being as therapeutics. Graphene nanomaterials are vastly used due to their unique properties and hence, lead to significantly exposure to humans and the environment. They shows toxicity in many fields including biomedical applications [262] and hence, toxicity effects of graphene nanomaterials in various cell have been summarized in Table 7 . A study has proved that graphene oxide and graphene destroy the lipid membrane after coming in direct contact with the human cell membrane, which comes out to be very dangerous. Hence, it is essential to estimate their degree and level of cytocompatibility and then, biocompatibility to address human health risks and environmental effects of graphene nanomaterials. Excessive use of reducing agents such as hydrazine hydrate or hydroquinone led to high rate of toxicity during conversion of graphene oxide to reduced graphene oxide. Among all graphene nanomaterials, graphene sheets have come out to be the best for use in biomedical applications with low risks. It has also been proved that reduced graphene sheets are less toxic as compared to the untreated graphene sheets. Excess uses of anti-cancer drugs delivery may cause serious injury, which results in even death or can resist future drugs actions and effects. Potentially effective methods to reduce toxicity are functionalization, enhanced water solubility, and the creation of novel compounds of graphene nanomaterials.

To overcome all the toxicity and dangerous points of graphene nanomaterials, various following steps have been taken:
1)
Green routes have to be followed for synthesis of graphene nanomaterials to minimize the toxicity level as it is eco-friendly [273].
2)
Ascorbic acid or L-ascorbic acid has been used to produce graphene sheets that are less toxic among all the graphene nanomaterials and prevent the agitation of graphene sheets, making them stable [274].
3)
Further, biocompatible polymers can be used to cover the surface of graphene nanomaterials for their practical application. It helps monitor the problems easily and accurately for both in vivo and in vitro systems. Polymer coating also plays a huge role in decreasing the toxicity of graphene nanomaterials [275]. The coating increases the stability, retention time in the bloodstream, solubility, and dispersity, which may enhance diagnosis and treatment efficacy in human health [276].
4)
PEGylated graphene nanomaterials have been used for anti-cancer treatments or chemotherapies, as it prevents tumor growth.

Graphene nanomaterials have attracted so much attention owing to their outstanding performance. This paper summarizes synthesis method, structure, properties, cytocompatibility, biocompatibility, and the final biological application of graphene nanomaterials. Drug delivery, gene delivery, bioimaging, tissue engineering, and biosensor applications of graphene nanomaterials have been discussed in detail. Graphene nanomaterials are a new carrier type due to their enormous specific surface area and unique arrangement, which give excellent adsorption capacity. The biosensing application of graphene nanomaterials is because of their high conductivity. Functionalizing graphene nanomaterials can improve water solubility, targeting, and biocompatibility properties. However, the clinical use of graphene nanomaterials is hampered by their in vivo and in vitro potential toxicity, such as protein adsorption and hemolysis. Potentially effective methods to reduce toxicity are functionalization, enhanced water solubility, and the synthesis of novel composites of graphene nanomaterials. The use of graphene nanomaterials in biomedical applications is expanding quickly. However, the technology is not mature enough, and hence, various therapeutic application requires solutions,
1.
In majority of cases, sensitivity of graphene nanomaterial in biomedical applications is far better than conventional methods. However, there are still significant differences between sensor production batches of them, which has a negative impact on detection. Hence, comprehensive details on the synthesis, purification, and characterization techniques of graphene nanomaterials should be offered and validated before actual deployment in biomedical applications.
2.
Graphene nanomaterials being inorganic materials are difficult to undergo self-degradation under physiological conditions compared to traditional biodegradable organic materials. There are various reports in vitro cytocompatibility of graphene nanomaterials and few reports on in vivo biocompatibility. Hence, improving their in vivo long-term toxicity is crucial to strengthen their usage in biomedical applications.
3.
The primary issue for graphene nanomaterials in their drug delivery and therapeutic applications is to methodically establish the pharmacokinetic properties and investigate the mechanism of in vivo transmembrane transport. It is challenging to accomplish as their physiochemical properties, including size, shape, chemical structure, and purity, depend on a particular form. Hence, it is challenging to produce standardized graphene nanomaterials with stable and controlled size, morphology, thickness, chemical structure, and purity.
4.
The main challenge for graphene nanomaterials in terms of their tissue engineering, antibacterial, and cell culture application is standardization of technology for large-scale commercialization. The relationship between the mechanism of cell differentiation and antibacterial agents with graphene nanomaterials must be understood, along with the distinctions between various graphene nanomaterials.
5.
Graphene nanomaterials composites can also act as potential smart biomaterials for 4D bioprinting. The selection of right bioink is main challenging step in 4D bioprinting. One of the most promising smart bioinks combine either inert nanoparticles and stimuli-responsive hydrogels or stimuli-responsive nanoparticles and inert hydrogels smart nanocomposite hybrid platforms.

In conclusion, graphene nanomaterials have a very promising and enthusiastic future in the field of biomedical applications. After overcoming the aforementioned difficulties, there is reason to anticipate that the restrictions on their use may soon be lifted.

Harshdeep Kaur: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Rahul Garg: Data curation. Sajan Singh: Data curation. Atanu Jana: Formal analysis. Chinna Bathula: Data curation.
